category_title: Students who engage in active instructional avoidance through disruptive and off-task behaviors
minimal_context: Martin is participating in a small-group reading activity with his peers and is expected to read his assigned materials quietly.
dialogue:
TUTOR: Let's start our small-group reading time. Please open your assigned materials and read the first page quietly.

STUDENT: (Turns his body completely away from the assigned reading materials)

TUTOR: Martin, you need to start reading.

STUDENT: (Gets out of his seat and leans over to the peer next to him) Hey, what are you doing after school?

TUTOR: Please sit down and focus on your work.

STUDENT: (Stays out of his seat, ignoring the assigned materials, and continues talking to his peer) I don't want to do this reading right now.

options.A: Ask the student what they notice about the assigned reading materials or how they know it is nonfiction.
options.B: Ensure a break card is kept on the student's desk as a prompt to take a break when needed.
options.C: Respond by offering enthusiastic praise, expressing pride that the student has finished another page of the reading already.
options.D: Nod or try to maintain eye contact with the student for as long as possible during the reading activity.
